home.bannerSubtitle: Discover grape varieties and grow your wine knowledge

seasonAlert.title: High downy mildew risk
seasonAlert.message: Rain and heat expected this week. Keep an eye on your leaves.

mildiou.name: Downy mildew
mildiou.description: Downy mildew is caused by the fungus Plasmopara viticola. It attacks all green parts of the vine, mainly the leaves.
mildiou.symptom1: Oily yellow spots on the upper surface of leaves
mildiou.symptom2: White cottony down on the underside
mildiou.treatment: Preventive copper-based treatment (Bordeaux mixture). Apply before rain, renew every 10-14 days.
mildiou.season: May to August — favored by heat and humidity

oidium.name: Powdery mildew
oidium.description: Powdery mildew is caused by Erysiphe necator. It develops in warm, dry weather, unlike downy mildew.
oidium.symptom1: White-grey powder on leaves and clusters
oidium.symptom2: Berries that crack or dry out
oidium.treatment: Sulfur dusting or spraying. Preventive treatments from bud break.
oidium.season: April to September — favored by warm, dry weather

blackRot.name: Black rot
blackRot.description: Black rot is caused by Guignardia bidwellii. It causes significant damage to berries.
blackRot.symptom1: Circular brown spots bordered with black on leaves
blackRot.treatment: Remove mummified berries. Preventive fungicide treatments in spring.
blackRot.season: May to July — favored by spring rains

esca.name: Esca
esca.description: Esca is a complex of wood diseases caused by several fungi. A chronic disease that can kill the vine.
esca.treatment: No curative treatment. Cutting back affected vine. Protect pruning wounds.
esca.season: Symptoms visible in summer — June to September

botrytis.name: Botrytis
botrytis.description: Grey rot is caused by Botrytis cinerea. It attacks clusters at maturity.
botrytis.treatment: Promote cluster aeration. Leaf removal. Anti-botrytis treatments before cluster closure.
botrytis.season: August to harvest — favored by humidity

flavescence.name: Flavescence dorée
flavescence.description: Phytoplasma disease transmitted by the leafhopper Scaphoideus titanus. Regulated disease, mandatory reporting.
flavescence.symptom1: Leaf rolling with yellow or red coloration depending on variety
flavescence.treatment: Mandatory uprooting of contaminated vines. Insecticide treatment against the vector leafhopper.

chlorose.name: Iron chlorosis
chlorose.description: Leaf yellowing due to iron deficiency, often linked to overly calcareous soil.
chlorose.symptom1: Yellowing between veins, veins remaining green
chlorose.treatment: Iron chelate application. Choose rootstock adapted to calcareous soils.
chlorose.season: Spring — especially on calcareous soils after heavy rain
